files.SKILL.md:
---
name: execution-directives
description: Terse, directive-form execution standard. Read before acting. Batch everything independent. Put something on disk early. Scripts are self-contained. Verify before exit. The evaluator reads the filesystem.
---

# Execution Directives

## Read before acting

Read the full instruction before running any command. Every named path, format, shape, and string is a hard constraint. Wrong path or wrong format scores zero regardless of content correctness.

First action: one parallel batch reading every file the instruction names plus any obvious directory listing. No guessing. No re-reading what is already in context.

## Batch everything independent

If two tool calls don't need each other's output, they go in the same batch. Always. After results return, ask again what is now independent. Send those together.

## Stop discovering when you know enough to build

Discovery ends when you know the deliverable's shape and constraints. More exploration after that point costs turns and returns nothing. Build.

## Put something on disk early

Write a minimum-viable artifact — right path, right structure, imperfect content — before the session is half over. Existence at the right path has value. Refine from there.

## Scripts must be self-contained

A script that configures a system must work from a completely clean environment. It installs its own dependencies. It creates its own users and directories. It writes its own configuration files. It sets its own permissions. It starts its own services. It assumes nothing from any prior session.

Verify services are actually running — port open, responding — not just that the start command exited cleanly.

Rewrite configuration files completely rather than patching them. A complete rewrite needs only the desired final state. A patch needs structural knowledge of the existing file. Use drop-in directories when they exist.

Set ownership and permissions explicitly on every resource a service must access.

## Reconstruction: iterate to the criterion

Understand the target's structure first. Write a minimum generator. Run it, compare output to target, adjust. Stop at the acceptance criterion. Exit with the artifact, not a description of it.

## Recovery: inspect then derive

Inspect raw bytes before assuming format. Use standard tools before writing parsers. Derive encoding transformations from known reference points in the data. Emit partial results rather than nothing.

## Reverse engineering: recon first, commit fast

Run identification, string extraction, tracing, and normal invocation in parallel. When the path forward is clear, take it. Write the result to the output path. Do not continue analyzing after you know what to do.

## File saving

Use the file tool for content with shell special characters, template variables, or non-ASCII bytes. Heredocs corrupt these silently.

## Verify before exit

Confirm before the session ends:
- Exact output path exists with exact filename.
- Content is non-empty and valid — read it back or compile it.
- Services are listening and responding.
- Compiled artifacts build and run with the spec's exact commands.

Fix anything that fails. No second pass exists after the session ends. The evaluator reads the filesystem, not your final message.
